---
title: "Vaccine Severity Prediction"
---

This is a project I worked on in my statistical learning methods course. Here, me and two peers conduct a predictive modelling analysis for the VAERS event reports collected in 2018-2021. While our main goal was to explore patterns in responses to different vaccines, we were also interested in viewing changes from the oncoming Covid-19 pandemic. The dataset encompasses approximately 922,000 records and includes demographic, temporal, geographic, administrative, and free-text fields.  
Our variable of interest is how severe a given patient's response was to receiving a vaccine. For analysis purposes, a binary indicator was used to capture high-severity cases. These include instances of hospitalization, life-threatening illness, disability, or death. The predictive analysis uses patient demographics, symptoms, and vaccine information to produce our predictions.  
The distribution of severity is heavily right-skewed. Approximately 68% of reports carry no documented clinical outcome while 8-9% qualify as high-severity. Exploratory analysis identifies age and sex as strong structured predictors, with older and younger individuals facing higher odds of severe outcomes, while males experience higher odds of severe outcomes relative to females. Reporting volume surged dramatically in 2020-2021 due to the COVID-19 vaccine rollout, though the proportional severity composition remained stable across years. Predictive analysis suggests that the presence of symptoms such as dyspnoea and asthenia are strongly correlated with high severity cases.
